main_event: Vivid blue sphere with tail flew south to north over Seattle and exploded in brilliant flash at approximately 7,000 feet at 0124 PST on 11 May 1952.
sighted_object: vivid blue sphere with tail; steep dive; flaming sparks or streamers; red body with trailing streaks; burst into flaming fragments at about 8,000 feet; chain lightning appearance on disintegration
witness_description: Northwest Airlines pilot Capt. B.C. Carlson and Co-Pilot Earl Perry; Seattle Police; Washington State Patrol; Sea-point Naval Tower personnel; weather observer Sidney Howick at Seattle-Tacoma airport; Airway Operations Specialist Robert Wiley (control tower); Don E. Lyman, Seattle engineer; University of Washington professor; many ground observers
interesting_points: Object traveled approximately 1600 to 3000 ft altitude, vivid blue, flaming sparks or streamers, came from south to north nearly straight down
Not picked up on radar; Col. Bennett: angle rather than speed probably caused no radar return
Capt. Carlson at 7000 ft: object exploded slightly below A/C level with lingering flash; shattered into 8 pieces looking like chain lightning
Don E. Lyman: flash at 1:24 a.m., 9 seconds duration, mercury or magnesium blue color; tremor started 2½ minutes after flash, explosion estimated 30-31 miles away
Robert Wiley, airport control tower: land tremor (explosion) followed about 2 minutes afterward
Two University of Washington astronomers contacted: stated object was a meteor; search for fragments initiated
Explosion time from Controller's log: 0035 PST; concussion felt as far as North Tacoma
